coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and testing of carbon quantum dots loaded 2D Bi2MoO6 for efficient Hg0 photocatalytic removal
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yili
Zhang, Hanlin
Shu, Yixiang
Zhao, Yongchun
Wang, Xuebin
Xiao, Rihong
Zhang, Junying
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was prepared and supported on 2D Bi2MoO6 by hydrothermal method to prepare 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite photocatalyst with up-conversion luminescence (UPCL) characteristics. The properties of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs were characterized by a series of characterization tools. Then, 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts were applied to photocatalytic Hg0 removal. The experimental results show that Hg0 removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs increased from 30.5% to 80.1% under visible light and from 7.2% to 26.4% under near-infrared light compared with 2D Bi2MoO6. The photostability test shows that 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs nanocomposites could maintain high photostability after five photoreaction cycles. UPCL test proved that CQDs had the ability to absorb infrared light and emit visible light, thus expanding the light utilization range of composite catalyst. Moreover, in N2+6%O2+12%CO2+NO atmosphere, Hg0 removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs first dropped and then raised with the increase of NO concentration due to the simultaneous reduction and oxidation of NO in the system. Finally, the ESR test shows that the modification of CQDs could not only enhance light absorption rang of photocatalyst, but also promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers on the catalyst surface, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic Hg0 removal capability of the 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Hg0 removal
2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs
UPCL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts.
(a)Full XPS spectrum of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2; (b)C 1s high resolution XPS spectrum of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2; O 1s high resolution XPS spectrum of (c)2D Bi2MoO6 and (d)2D Bi2MoO6-C2
SEM images of 2D Bi2MoO6 (a) and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 (b, c) ; TEM (d) and HRTEM (e, f) imagesof 2D Bi2MoO6-C2.
(a)UV–vis DRS spectra; (b)transient photocurrent density; (c) EIS Nyquist plot and (d) UPCL properties of CQDs (excitation wavelength: 980 nm)
Photocatalytic mercury removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C samples under(a) visible light and (b) near-infrared light
the influence of SO2 and NO on the characteristics of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 for removing elemental mercury
(a)DMPO ·OH and ·O2 - ESR signals of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst; (b)Schematic diagram of photogenerated carrier transfer of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 catalyst.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and testing of carbon quantum dots loaded 2D Bi2MoO6 for efficient Hg0 photocatalytic removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Huazhong University of Science & Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Huazhong University of Science & Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was prepared and supported on 2D Bi2MoO6 by hydrothermal method to prepare 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite photocatalyst with up-conversion luminescence (UPCL) characteristics. The properties of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs were characterized by a series of characterization tools. Then, 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts were applied to photocatalytic Hg0 removal. The experimental results show that Hg0 removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs increased from 30.5% to 80.1% under visible light and from 7.2% to 26.4% under near-infrared light compared with 2D Bi2MoO6. The photostability test shows that 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs nanocomposites could maintain high photostability after five photoreaction cycles. UPCL test proved that CQDs had the ability to absorb infrared light and emit visible light, thus expanding the light utilization range of composite catalyst. Moreover, in N2+6%O2+12%CO2+NO atmosphere, Hg0 removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs first dropped and then raised with the increase of NO concentration due to the simultaneous reduction and oxidation of NO in the system. Finally, the ESR test shows that the modification of CQDs could not only enhance light absorption rang of photocatalyst, but also promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers on the catalyst surface, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic Hg0 removal capability of the 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is a toxic substance, which has caused serious harm to human health and ecological environment. Coal combustion is one of the main sources of atmospheric mercury, so it is significant to research and develop mercury removal technology for coal-fired flue gas [1,2]. There are three main forms of mercury in coal-fired flue gas: particulate mercury (Hgp), divalent mercury (Hg2+) and elemental mercury (Hg0). The existing pollutant control facilities can capture most of Hg2+ and Hgp. Hg2+ is very soluble in water and easily attached to the solid particles in the flue gas, which could be directly dissolved into the slurry after passing through WFGD device. The removal of Hgp is simpler, because the particulate matter will be directly captured by the electrostatic precipitator device. However, Hg0 is hard to dissolve in water and is highly volatile, so it is difficult to directly control it through existing pollutant control equipment [3,4]. Therefore, Hg0 is the most hard mercury form to control among the three forms [5], which also become the main target of the current mercury removal technology.

Photocatalytic oxidation is a technology that uses light energy to excite semiconductor catalyst to achieve oxidation effect, which could oxidize Hg0 to Hg2+ and then is removed by WFGD device. However, the most widely used Ti based semiconductors have large band gaps and can only produce photocatalytic activity under UV irradiation [6–8]. Bismuth based catalyst is a kind of visible photocatalyst whose photocatalytic performance is much better than that of common nano catalysts. As a member of Aurivillius family, Bi2MoO6 with the layered bismuth oxide family are of special interest due to their dielectric, ion-conductive, luminescent and catalytic properties [9,10]. Recent results revealed that Bi2MoO6 may be a potential candidate as an excellent photocatalyst and solar-energy-conversion material for water splitting and degradation of organic compounds under visible-light irradiation [11,12]. However, when illuminated under visible light irradiation, the relatively high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons/holes pairs for pure Bi2MoO6 hinders its widely application. It is of great theoretical and practical meaning to explore its mercury removal performance. Researchers are committed to using a variety of modification methods to improve its performance.

Noble metallic and non-metallic loading is also a feasible modification method. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nano catalysts with a size less than 10 nm. In recent years, they have received a lot of attention owing to their low price, non-toxic, richness as well as environmental friendliness. CQDs had electron library transfer property, which could effectively inhibit the charge recombination rate of CQDs/semiconductor composite catalyst [13–16]. In addition to the same optical characteristics as general semiconductors, CQDs also have unique up-conversion luminescence (UPCL) characteristics [17–20]. Cao et al. [20] observed that the CQD produced by laser ablation showed strong luminescence under near-infrared (800 nm) excitation. In 2010, Li et al. [21] used CQDs and TiO2 or SiO2 to synthesize the CQDs-based photocatalyst with good dye degradation performance for the first time. Its photocatalysis mechanism is that CQDs absorb visible light and emit UV light, so as to promote TiO2 or SiO2 to generate photogenerated carriers, and then react with the adsorbed ·O2 and ·OH- to generate the active free radicals required for the photocatalytic reaction. This exciting feature makes it possible for CQDs/semiconductor composite photocatalyst to use longer near-infrared and infrared light.

In this work, 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs nano catalyst was synthesized, and the Hg0 removal capability of the CQDs modifiled catalyst under visible and near-infrared light were explored. The physical and chemical characteristics of the catalyst were investigated through a number of characterization tests.

CQDs was fabricated in the following steps. 3 g of citric acid was placed into 10 ml of deionized water and stirred magnetically for 1h. And then, the liquid was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor and heated at 180℃ for 5 h. When it naturally cooled to room temperature, a brown aqueous solution was obtained. The solution was filtered out of large particles through a centrifuge, and the remaining solution was transferred to a freeze dryer for 24 h at low temperature to obtain brown black powder.

2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite catalyst was fabricated via hydrothermal method. The CQDs powder with a mass of 350 mg was dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water to obtain an aqueous solution of CQDs with a concentration of 25 mg/ml. 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 2 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O, 900 mg cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) powder, and CQDs aqueous solutions with different volumes were added into 30 mL of 0.1M HNO3 and 30 mL of deionized water respectively and was sonicated for 1 h. The resulting aqueous suspension was transferred into a PTFE-lined reactor with a capacity of 200 mL, which was heated to 180℃ with temperature rise rate of 10℃/min for 24 h. Other steps remained unchanged. The amounts of the added CQDs aqueous solutions were 0 mL, 1.5 mL, 3.0 mL and 4.5 mL, respectively, and the obtained catalysts were labeled as 2D Bi2MoO6, 2D Bi2MoO6-C1, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C3, respectively.

The phase types of the samples was tested by a BRUKER D8 ADVANCE Diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (k=1.5406 Å, 40 mA and 40 kV) and the scanning range was 20-70° (2θ). The surface microstructure of the catalyst was observed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, FEI company of the Netherlands, sirion200) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2F30). The surface compositions were explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which was carried out on PHI5300 instrument with Al Ka X-ray (hm = 1486.6 eV) radiation. UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra can be used to test the light absorption intensity of the catalyst. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is utilized to measure the recombination level of photoexcited electrons and holes in semiconductor materials. The equipment used in this study is FLS980 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer, produced by Edinburgh instruments, UK. Photocurrent response (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested at Shanghai Chenhua electrochemical workstation (CHI760D). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped oxidative radicals were obtained on a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

The catalytic activities were evaluated using a bench-scale experimental system which is similar to our previous works [22]. The baseline (BL) flue gas, which only contained 6% O2, 12% CO2, and balance N2. For the sake of testing the effect of flue gas components, SO2 and NO of different concentrations would be introduced. The nitrogen with total flow rate of 1.2 L min−1 which was divided into two branches in one of which 150 mL/min passed through the U-shape glass Hg0 permeating tube heated at immobile water-bath temperature (60°C) to provide for the reaction system with stable Hg0 stationary concentration (70 µg/m3). The nitrogen flow rates were controlled by mass flow meters (MFC, CS200 type). The other stream has a 1.05 L/min flow rate. An on-line mercury analyzer (RA-915-M, Lumex Ltd., Russia) based on atomic absorption spectrometry was used to measure the gas phase concentration of Hg0. Under the 248 WM/cm2 light source irradiation of Xenon lamp, 20mg catalyst was evenly placed on the quartz sand core for photocatalytic performance test. The Hg0 removal capability of the catalyst can be determined by the change of elemental mercury concentration before and after through the catalyst. Finally, in order to prevent air pollution after mercury containing gas was discharged from the system, the tail gas was introduced into the activated carbon containing treatment bottle for purification. Hg0 removal efficiency can be calculated using the following equation:
η Hg
=
∑
0
t
Hg
in
0
-
∑
0
t
Hg
out
0
∑
0
t
Hg
in
0
×
100\%
where
Hg
in
0
and
Hg
out
0
represent Hg0 concentrations of inlet and outlet of the photocatalytic reactor, µg/m3, respectively.

The XRD patterns of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite photocatalysts with different loading amounts are shown in Fig. 1 . All diffraction peaks were entirely suitable to orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 phase (JCPDS No. 97-001-4266) [23]. The XRD diffraction peaks of all crystal planes of the samples were sharp and strong, indicating that the prepared catalyst had good crystallinity. The purity of the sample was relatively high, and no diffraction peaks of other substances were found. However, no characteristic peaks of carbon quantum dots were observed on the XRD patterns of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite catalysts, which could be attributed to the low content of loaded CQDs and its small particles. This phenomenon also indicated that the original crystal structures of 2D Bi2MoO6 had not changed after CQDs loading. The diffraction peak of composite material 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs was weaker than that of pure 2D Bi2MoO6, which can be attributed to the coverage of CQDs on the surface of 2D Bi2MoO6 to some extent, indicating that there was CQDs in the composite. In addition, no diffraction peaks of other impurity phases were detected, which proved that no other substances were formed.

Comparing the XPS full spectrum of 2D Bi2MoO6 catalyst before and after CQDs loading (Fig. 2 a), in addition to the typical characteristic peaks of Bi, Mo and O [24], it can be clearly observed that the intensity of the characteristic peak of C1s in 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 increased, which verified the successful loading of CQDs on 2D Bi2MoO6 surface. Fig. 2b reveals that 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 contain a sp3 carbon peak with a hydroxyl group at 285.4 eV, a carbonyl group at 286.4 eV and a carboxylic acid group at 288.5 eV, while the sp2 carbon peak mainly located at 284.6 eV with 50.2%, which illustrated that 49.8% carbon of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 was connected with oxygen functional groups, playing an important role in catalytic reaction. Fig. 2c shows the O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of 2D Bi2MoO6 catalyst before and after CQDs loading. In the binding energy range of 526-538 eV, the characteristic peak curve of O element could be divided into two peaks, and the characteristic peak at the higher binding energy may represent the more active surface active oxygen [25,26]. Compared with that before CQDs loading, the proportion of surface active oxygen in 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst increased significantly, and the proportion of active oxygen in 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 increased from 25.6% to 65.0% (Fig. 2d). Therefore, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 was activated to generate more superoxide free radicals after being irradiated by sunlight to participate in the photocatalytic Hg0 removal reaction.

Fig. 3 shows the morphology and microstructure of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 under scanning electron microscope (SEM). With the CTAB regulation, Bi2MoO6 became layered structure with the very thin thickness (Fig. 3a). After the CQDs were successfully loaded, the two-dimensional flakes was relatively more regular (Fig. 3b).

In order to confirm the successful loading of carbon quantum dots in the composite catalyst structure, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst was scanned by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). TEM image (Fig. 3c) further verifies that 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs was a sheet-like structure. In Fig. 3d, it was obvious that many small particles with a diameter of about 7-8 nm are observed on the surface of 2D Bi2MoO6 nanosheets respectively, and the particles were well dispersed. The lattice stripes on the small particles loaded on the surface were clearly visible, representing good crystallinity. Through calculation, it was concluded that the crystal plane spacing of the loaded particles on the surface of the composite catalyst was 0.32 nm, belonging to the (002) lattice plane of CQDs [27]. The successful loading of CQDs can optimize the transmission of photogenerated carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical and electrical properties
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts [28,29]. The band gap of 2D Bi2MoO6 was calculated to be 2.63 eV. There were obvious changes on the light absorption curves of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs composite catalysts under different amounts of CQDs loading. With the increase of CQDs content, the visible light absorbance intensities of the catalyst increased significantly, which showed that the introduction of CQDs could expand the light absorption response range of the catalyst and improve the solar energy utilization.

For the sake of further analyzing the photoexcited carreirs separation characteristics of the catalysts, a series of photo-electrochemical tools such as photocurrent response (PC) measurement, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy of 2D Bi2MoO6, 2D Bi2MoO6-C1, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C3 were also conducted. Fig. 4b shows that the photocurrent response intensity of 2D Bi2MoO6-C samples were 4–5 times that of 2D Bi2MoO6. The result demonstrated that photogenerated carriers of the photocatalyst supported by CQDs were easier to separate than that of 2D Bi2MoO6. Generally speaking, the smaller were radius represented the smaller impendence, implying the higher the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers [30,31]. Moreover, in comparison with 2D Bi2MoO6, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 displayed the smallest arc radius in Fig. 4c, indicating the resistance on transfer process of the photoinduced electrons and holes was declined distinctly [32]. The reduction of impedance well-confirms more efficient carriers separation as well as faster interfacial charge transfer efficiency in the 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts. In order to prove the change of up-conversion luminescence (UPCL) properties of 2D Bi2MoO6 catalysts after CQDs loading, Fig. 4d shows the emission spectra of CQDs under the excitation wavelength of 980 nm. The test results show that CQDs could convert the light source in the wavelength of 980 nm to the light in the wavelength range of 400-500 nm, indicating that CQDs owned the UPCL characteristics. Under the irradiation of light, the photogenerated electrons generated on the CQDs surface were first excited to the excited state, and the excited electrons continue to transition to higher energy orbits. Finally, the electrons in the high energy state returned to the ground state, and the carriers recombined, Thus, light with higher energy than the excitation wavelength was emitted [33]. This feature of CQDs was conducive to expand the utilization of light source and promote the catalytic reaction of 2D Bi2MoO6.

Fig. 5 a shows that there were two different trends in the removal efficiency of Hg0 under the conditions of N2+6%O2+12%CO2 atmosphere. No obvious decrease in the concentration of elemental mercury was observed in all catalysts without light in five minutes, indicating that the subsequent mercury removal effect of the catalyst came from the action of light. Upon visible-light irradiation, the efficiency of photocatalytic Hg0 removal rose rapidly and tended to be stable. The Hg0 removal efficiencies of 2D Bi2MoO6, 2D Bi2MoO6-C1, 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C3 were 30.5%, 59.6%, 80.1% and 60.2%, respectively. With the increase of CQDs loading content, the efficiency of photocatalytic mercury removal of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDs catalysts increased first and then descended, in which the Hg0 removal efficiency (80.1%) of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 reached the maximum. Further increasing CQDs loading (2D Bi2MoO6-C3) was detrimental to the performance possibly because the overloading CQDs shielded the light absorption of Bi2MoO6. The results prove that proper CQDs loading could effectively improve the visible-light photocatalytic Hg0 removal performance of 2D Bi2MoO6. In addition, Hg0 removal experiments of 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 catalysts were carried out under the light with wavelength above 700 nm. The experimental data (Fig. 5b) show that before loading CQDs, the catalyst had almost no mercury removal effect under the irradiation of near-infrared and infrared light. However, after loading CQDs, the mercury removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst under near-infrared and infrared light reached 26.4%. Therefore, it was proved that the introduction of CQDs can make the composite catalyst sensitive to the near-infrared spectrum of sunlight and improve the utilization of sunlight. The reaction process could be described by the following reactions:
hν→h++e-
H2O→H++OH-
O2+e-→·O2 -
H2O+h+→·OH+H+
OH-+h+→·OH
2·O2 -+3Hg0+2H+→3HgO+H2O
2·OH+Hg0→HgO+H2O

The influence of SO2 and NO on the characteristics of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst for removing elemental mercury from simulated gas was explored, as shown in Fig. 6 . Under the conditions of N2+O2+CO2 atmosphere and visible light, SO2 with concentrations of 400, 800 and 1200 ppm was introduced respectively. In the presence of 400 ppm SO2, the mercury removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst decreased from 80.1% to 63.5%. And then, with a continued increase in the SO2 concentration to 800 ppm and 1200 ppm, the Hg0 removal efficiency basically unchanged. Therefore, the 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 material exhibited a weak reaction to SO2 and demonstrated high resistance to a high concentration SO2 atmosphere. When the concentration of NO continued to increase to 100 and 150 ppm, the Hg0 concentration at the outlet of the photocatalytic reaction system increased, indicating that Hg0 removal efficiency of CQDs supported catalysts decreased. However, when the NO concentration continued to increased to 100 and 150 ppm, the Hg0 concentration gradually descended. This phenomenon was different from that before. When there was NO and O2 in the gas, NO would be rapidly oxidized to NO2. When mercury with weak metal-bond coexisted with strong oxidants such as NO2 and O2, it could interact with them and converted into HgO. When the content of NO was low, the concentration of produced NO2 was correspondingly low. Due to the presence of C element and some hydroxyl functional groups in the CQDs, the reduction reaction of NO took place in a large proportion to inhibit Hg0 removal. With the increase of NO concentration, the proportion of NO in oxidation reaction was greater than that in reduction reaction, which made the Hg0 removal efficiency of composite catalyst rise. Possible reaction pathways were as follows:
NO +
O 2
→
NO 2
+ O
NO 2
+ NO +
Bi 2
WO 6
- C
1
/
Bi 2
MoO 6
- C
2
→
N 2
+
CO 2
+
H 2
O
Hg
0
+ O → HgO
Hg0+ 2O +2NO2→Hg(NO3)2

The stability of a photocatalyst is also significant from point of view for its practical application. As shown in Fig. 7 b, five cycles was performed to test the stability of the 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 sample under visible light irradiation. After five tests, the Hg0 removal efficiency of 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 was 78.2%. The results explained that 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite catalyst had high photocyclic stability.

ESR test was used to determine the active substances on 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 photocatalyst. The results are shown in Fig. 8 a. Under visible light irradiation, the characteristic peaks of DMPO ·OH and ·O2 - species could be observed in 2D Bi2MoO6 and 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 dispersion respectively. ·OH and ·O2 - were all effective active substances. And after CQDs loading, the intensity of the two free radicals increased. This was because after loaded by CQDs, the photogenerated electrons generated on the surface of 2D Bi2MoO6 catalyst excited by visible light were transferred to the CQDs surface, which reduced the recombination rate of photogenerated hole-electron pairs of the composite catalyst. Therefore, the concentration of free radicals detected by ESR increased. Based on the above experimental data, the mechanism diagram of photocatalytic Hg0 oxidation by 2D Bi2MoO6-C2 composite photocatalyst was drawn in Fig. 8b. Hg0 can easily be oxidized by the existing ·OH and ·O2 - to HgO.

CQDs were deposited on 2D Bi2MoO6 nanosheets successfully by a hydrothermal method in this work. The results show that an appropriate amount of CQDs loading would improve mercury removal efficiencies of 2D Bi2MoO6 nanosheets up from 30.5% to 80.1% and from 7.2% to 26.4% under visible light and near-infrared light, respectively. After compounding with 2D Bi2MoO6, CQDs not only narrowed the optical band gap, but also provided trap positions (acceptor states) for photogenerated electrons and enhanced the charge separation process, which would improve the photocatalytic performance of 2D Bi2MoO6/CQDS. In addition, the composite catalyst had UPCL characteristics, which could absorb infrared and near-infrared light and convert it into visible light, so as to improve the light utilization range of the catalyst and enhance its photocatalytic mercury removal efficiency. This research may furnish new perspective into the development of high-efficiency bismuth based catalysts.

Yili Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision. Hanlin Zhang: Software, Validation. Yixiang Shu: . Yongchun Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Xuebin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Rihong Xiao: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision. Junying Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2019YFC1907000), the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (2018QYTD05), the Key Research and Development Program of Hubei Province (2020BCA076) and Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2021JC-03).

International Journal of Coal Geology
